rationale: In the theory development, Zhao et al. (2007) is cited as part of the key prior work distinguishing process-focused versus outcome-focused mental simulation. In the discussion, the authors directly compare their results with Zhao et al., stating that Zhao found construal-shifting effects for familiar choices, whereas their new-product context did not show that pattern. They use this contrast to argue a boundary condition based on product familiarity and uncertainty, giving the citation a clear conceptual role beyond background mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Zhao et al.'s mental-simulation-over-time framework but qualifies its scope. It argues that in high-uncertainty new product adoption, matching simulation type to temporal concerns (process-near, outcome-distant) is more effective, refining when Zhao et al.'s effects should be expected.
